the gap how it works what they found does it hold up? takeaways
2024 · ACL 2024 · Benchmarks & Evals

Evaluating Very Long-Term Conversational Memory of LLM Agents

Prior long-dialogue benchmarks stop at five chat sessions. LoCoMo builds 50 synthetic-but-human-edited conversations averaging 300 turns and 19.3 sessions spread over months, then tests recall, causal/temporal reasoning, and persona consistency across three tasks. The best model tested, GPT-4-turbo, reaches just 32.1 F1 on the question-answering task against a human score of 87.9.

♪ listen — audio episode
3-voice deep dive · mp3 ↗ · rss feed ↗
Adyasha Maharana, Dong-Ho Lee, Sergey Tulyakov, Mohit Bansal, Francesco Barbieri, Yuwei Fang · UNC Chapel Hill, USC, Snap Inc.
venue ACL 2024 citations 639 topic Benchmarks & Evals source ↗ listen ♪
// citation count as of 2026-07-18
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

Every prior long-dialogue benchmark quietly capped how long "long" was allowed to be. MPChat and MMDialog are single-turn, image-grounded exchanges averaging 53–73 tokens. DailyDialog and SODA run one session of 7–8 turns. Even the datasets built specifically to study multi-session memory — MSC (Xu et al., 2022), whose sessions are spaced at most a few days apart, and Conversation Chronicles (Jang et al., 2023), whose sessions can be spread anywhere from a few hours to years apart — max out at 4–5 sessions and 53–59 turns per conversation. The paper's framing is blunt: "existing works on long-term open-domain dialogues focus on evaluating model responses within contexts spanning no more than five chat sessions." Nobody had tested whether an LLM agent can track a relationship, a persona, and a chain of life events across dozens of sessions and real calendar months — which is closer to how people actually use long-running chat assistants.

The standard toolkit for scoring dialogue agents doesn't fix this either. Lexical overlap (BLEU), semantic overlap (BERTScore), consistency and contradiction detection, and empathy scoring all evaluate a single response against a single piece of context — they say nothing about whether an agent actually integrated something said 20 sessions and three months ago. LoCoMo's contribution is two-fold: a generation pipeline that can produce dialogues an order of magnitude longer than anything before it, and an evaluation suite built specifically to probe memory at that timescale rather than local response quality.

// how it works

The method

LoCoMo is built, not collected — a two-stage machine-human pipeline that first generates a very long conversation between two persona-driven LLM agents, then has human annotators clean it up.

Persona and temporal event graph

Each of two virtual agents starts from a short persona seed drawn from the MSC dataset (4–5 sentences), which gpt-3.5-turbo expands into a fuller persona covering objectives, past experience, daily habits, relationships, and basic demographics. On top of the persona, the pipeline builds a temporal event graph G: up to 25 life events per agent, each with a date and causal edges to other events, generated iteratively in batches of 3 by conditioning text-davinci-003 on the persona, spread across a 6–12 month window. This event graph is what gives the conversation a plot: in the paper's worked example, a persona who aspires to be a hotel manager enrolls in a hotel management course that resurfaces months later as an excited social-media post, while a separate causal chain has that persona's passion for gaming lead to an invitation from a well-known gaming company — parallel, persona-driven threads rather than a single causal line.

Agent architecture and human editing

Each agent reuses the generative-agent architecture from Park et al. (2023): a reflect & respond loop conditions each turn on short-term memory (a running per-session summary wk) and long-term memory (per-turn "observations" okj retrieved from earlier sessions), plus the subset of event-graph events that fall between the previous and current session dates. A separate image sharing & reaction module lets agents generate a caption, convert it to search keywords, pull a matching image from the web, and react to images they receive (captioned via BLIP-2). Finally, human annotators edit the raw LLM output for long-range consistency, remove or substitute irrelevant images, and verify that dialogue stays grounded in the event graph — the authors report annotators edited ~15% of dialogue turns and removed or substituted ~19% of images. The output: 50 conversations, avg. 19.3 sessions and 304.9 turns each, 9,209.2 tokens on average, spanning "few months" per conversation.

Three evaluation tasks

On top of the 50 conversations, LoCoMo defines a benchmark of three tasks. Question answering covers 7,512 questions split into five reasoning categories — single-hop (2,705, 36%), multi-hop (1,104, 14.6%), temporal (1,547, 20.6%), open-domain/commonsense (285, 3.9%), and adversarial/unanswerable (1,871, 24.9%) — scored by F1 partial-match against gold answers lifted verbatim from the dialogue where possible. Event summarization asks a model to summarize events in a time window and compares the summary against the ground-truth event graph using an adapted FActScore (Min et al., 2023):

F1 = 2 · P · RP + R // P = share of the summary's atomic facts that match an event in G; R = share of G's atomic facts recovered by the summary

Multi-modal dialogue generation trains three variants of MiniGPT-5 (base; +session-summary context; +retrieved-observation context) on LoCoMo's raw (unedited) conversations and scores predicted image+text responses against the held-out ground truth using MM-Relevance (Feng et al., 2023) plus standard NLG metrics.

// what they found

The results

On question answering, humans score 87.9 F1 overall; every model tested falls far short. Among base (context-truncated) models, GPT-4-turbo leads at 32.1, ahead of GPT-3.5-turbo (22.4), Llama-2-Chat-70B (17.9), and Mistral-Instruct-7B (13.9). Feeding GPT-3.5-turbo-16K more context helps overall score — 24.1 at 4K rising to 37.8 at 16K — but the gains are lopsided: single-hop climbs from 31.7 to 56.4, while adversarial-question accuracy collapses from 13.1 to 2.1 as context grows, versus GPT-4-turbo's 70.2 on the same category with only a 4K window. Longer context makes the model better at recalling facts and worse at recognizing when a question has no answer.

Retrieval-augmented generation helps, but only when memory is chunked the right way. Retrieving from a database of per-turn "observations" (atomic assertions extracted from dialogue) beats retrieving raw dialogue turns or session summaries: overall F1 peaks at 41.4 with top-5 observations, versus 34.8 for top-50 raw dialogue turns and 32.5 for top-5 summaries — all well above the no-retrieval baseline of 22.4. Interestingly, performance on observations degrades past top-5 (down to 37.8 at top-50), which the authors attribute to falling signal-to-noise ratio as more retrieved context is added. Across both tables, temporal reasoning and open-domain knowledge questions are flagged as the hardest categories — RAG in particular hurts open-domain accuracy, since inaccurate retrieved context can override knowledge already encoded in the model's parameters.

On event summarization, GPT-3.5-turbo edges out GPT-4-turbo on F1 (45.9 vs. 45.1) despite GPT-4-turbo's higher precision (51.6 vs. 45.3) — and, counter-intuitively, the long-context GPT-3.5-turbo-16K variant underperforms the same base model's 4K-context version (39.9 vs. 45.9 F1), echoing the QA finding that more context window doesn't automatically mean better long-range comprehension. Manual error analysis surfaces five recurring failure types: missing information, hallucinated details, misreading dialogue tone (sarcasm/humor taken literally), wrong speaker attribution, and treating insignificant chatter as salient events. On multimodal dialogue generation, incorporating retrieved observations into MiniGPT-5's training context measurably improves persona-faithfulness, and MM-Relevance score drops as dialogue history grows longer — a drop that retrieval augmentation only partly offsets.

// does it hold up?

The eval read

LoCoMo correctly identifies a real, underserved gap — nobody was testing dialogue memory past five sessions — and the headline contrast (87.9 human F1 vs. 32.1 for the best model) is a legitimately dramatic result. But several construction choices mean the size of that gap should be read as directionally right rather than precisely calibrated.

Start with provenance. The conversations aren't scraped from the world; they're generated by gpt-3.5-turbo agents (event graphs by text-davinci-003) built on Park et al.'s generative-agent architecture, seeded from personas drawn from the public MSC dataset. Three of the five QA baselines evaluated — GPT-3.5-turbo, GPT-3.5-turbo-16K, and GPT-4-turbo — are from the same lab that generated the underlying text. That's not classic train/test contamination (the QA gold answers are separately annotated), but it does mean part of the leaderboard shares a stylistic and structural generation family with the benchmark itself, an advantage Mistral-7B and Llama-2-70B don't get.

Next, the human-verification layer is thinner than the paper's framing suggests. Only ~15% of dialogue turns and ~19% of images were touched by annotators — meaning the large majority of "very long-term" conversation content is raw, unverified LLM output. No inter-annotator agreement is reported for even that edited fraction: the paper's own appendix states annotators were in-house and their demographics "unable to be obtained... due to the confidential nature of such information," with no process metric offered in its place. The authors' Limitations section is admirably candid that the dataset "may not fully reflect the nuances of real-world online conversations" — worth taking at face value rather than reading past.

The metric layer inherits a known weakness. The paper's own Limitations section flags that "LLMs are prone to generating verbose answers even when prompted to answer in short phrases," creating exactly the F1-partial-match brittleness that Xu et al. (2023) — cited by the authors themselves — document as a general problem for long-form QA scoring. That brittleness bites hardest on the adversarial category, where a "correct" answer is typically a short "not answerable" span: the reported swing from 70.2% (GPT-4-turbo, 4K) down to 2.1% (GPT-3.5-turbo-16K) is a striking finding, but with no human-verified refusal check reported alongside it, some of that swing could be scoring-format sensitivity rather than pure capability. Similarly, the open-domain-knowledge category that partly anchors the "hardest scenario" claim rests on just 285 of 7,512 questions (3.9%) — the smallest slice by far — with no confidence intervals reported anywhere in the paper for any category.

baselines

Reasonable for a Feb-2024 snapshot (Mistral-7B, Llama-2-70B, gpt-3.5-turbo/-16k, gpt-4-turbo, one DRAGON-retriever RAG pipeline) but narrow — no dedicated long-context or memory-augmented architecture is compared, and three of five QA baselines share a lab with the data-generation pipeline.

contamination

Not classic pretraining leakage (dialogues are synthetic, gold answers separately annotated) — but personas are seeded from the public MSC dataset, and the same model family that generated the text also appears among its strongest evaluated baselines.

what's missed

No inter-annotator agreement for the human editing pass, despite ~85% of turns and ~81% of images being unedited LLM output; no confidence intervals anywhere, including for the 285-question (3.9%) open-domain category used to support a headline claim.

would raise confidence

Report IAA on the annotation/editing pass; add per-category error bars or bootstrap CIs; pair the F1-partial-match adversarial score with a human-verified refusal check; benchmark at least one long-context or memory-specific system outside the OpenAI family.

// bottom line

LoCoMo is a genuine and influential contribution — it made "memory across months, not minutes" a testable property instead of an assumption, and 635 citations in under two years suggests the field agreed the gap was real. But the specific numbers (87.9 vs. 32.1, or the adversarial 70.2-to-2.1 swing) sit downstream of a synthetic, thinly-audited, same-family-favoring pipeline and a metric the authors themselves flag as unreliable for LLM output — treat them as evidence that long-term memory is a real, large problem, not as a precise measurement of how large.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. The number that matters: 87.9 vs. 32.1. On LoCoMo's QA task, humans score 87.9 F1 overall; the best model tested, GPT-4-turbo, reaches only 32.1 — and the long-context model with the highest overall score (GPT-3.5-turbo-16K, 37.8) gets there while its adversarial-question accuracy collapses to 2.1%.
  2. Scale is the actual contribution. 50 conversations averaging 304.9 turns and 19.3 sessions over "few months" each — about 6× the turns and 4× the sessions (and ~9× the token length) of the next-largest prior multi-session dataset — produced by persona- and temporal-event-graph-conditioned LLM agents, then partially human-edited.
  3. Retrieval unit matters more than retrieval depth. Chunking memory as atomic per-turn "observations" and retrieving the top-5 lifts QA F1 to 41.4, beating raw-dialogue or session-summary retrieval at any depth — but performance degrades past top-5 as retrieved noise increases.
  4. Read the gap as directional, not exact. The dataset is generated by the same OpenAI model family that anchors three of five evaluated baselines, only ~15–19% of content was human-verified with no reported inter-annotator agreement, and the paper's own Limitations section flags its F1-based answer scoring as unreliable for LLM output.
